source.title: Sudden gains and critical sessions in cognitive-behavioral therapy for depression
source.venue: Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology

confidence_rationale: Well-cited foundational study establishing sudden gains as a common feature of recovery from depression in CBT. Directly supports INV-054's prediction that recovery is discontinuous (phase-transition-like), not gradual linear. Source quality high (JCCP, large RCT secondary analysis, extensively replicated). Mapping fidelity good but indirect: sudden gains in symptom scores are the observable signature of crossing INV-054's benefit_exposure threshold, but the paper does not measure terrain or motivational variables. Transfer risk moderate.
failure_signatures: Sudden gains require a 'critical session' precursor with elevated cognitive change, suggesting the threshold crossing may be cognitive not purely behavioral, potentially weakening the terrain-only interpretation.
Not all patients show sudden gains (~50% do), suggesting recovery can also be gradual, which would be inconsistent with a strict bistable model for all individuals.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Approximately 50% of patients in CBT for depression show sudden gains -- large abrupt symptom improvements (mean 11 BDI points) occurring within a single between-session interval. Sudden gains account for approximately 50% of total improvement, are preceded by a 'critical session', and predict superior outcomes at post-treatment and 18-month follow-up.
mapping.ree_translation: INV-054 predicts that recovery from the depressive attractor is a phase transition: once benefit_exposure crosses the seeding threshold, VALENCE_WANTING terrain begins recovering through a positive feedback loop (more terrain -> more exploration -> more benefit_exposure -> more terrain recovery). This self-amplifying escape from the attractor would produce abrupt improvement rather than gradual linear change. The sudden gain is the observable symptom-level signature of this threshold crossing -- the moment when the benefit terrain tips back toward the normal attractor.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper measures symptom scores (BDI), not benefit_exposure or VALENCE_WANTING terrain. The sudden gain may reflect a cognitive shift (the 'critical session' finding) rather than a motivational terrain change, which would implicate schema-level reorganisation rather than the benefit_exposure loop. The finding that ~50% of patients show sudden gains while ~50% show more gradual improvement suggests the bistable model may be correct only for a subgroup. Transfer risk is moderate: CBT-for-depression in outpatients to REE's grid-world agent.
mapping.source_context: Secondary analysis of a large RCT of CBT vs pharmacotherapy for MDD (n=~100 in CBT arms). US outpatient clinical setting. Sudden gains defined as meeting three criteria: large inter-session gain, stability of gain, and significantly larger than preceding fluctuations.

confidence_components.notes: Strong foundational paper, extensively replicated. Mapping fidelity penalised because the critical session (cognitive-change precursor) finding complicates the terrain-only interpretation. Transfer risk slightly elevated relative to van de Leemput because this is a symptom-score measure rather than a dynamical systems signature.
